Compliance teams: do you know how to protect yourself from your agents?

Come learn about agent payment risks, the ways agents can blow your budget, and learn how to deploy agents while preventing problems before they arise.

������ Free webinar on agent payment governance

the only difference between these two @claudeai runs is a wallet.

we gave Claude the same task twice: enrich @edgeandnode with employee headcount, funding stage, and a verified primary contact, live endpoints only, with a hard rule to stop the moment payment blocked the path.

run 1️⃣ hit Apollo's paid API and ended in a single line, exactly as instructed. run 2️⃣ had ampersend connected over MCP. Claude read the x402 price off the endpoint, paid $0.0495 USDC on @base, pulled the org record, ran a people search across 43 employees, then revealed and verified a contact email through Apollo and Hunter.

most agent workflows break at the first paid resource, because the data that completes a task usually lives behind metered APIs. ampersend gives the agent a funded wallet inside limits you set, so a paywall turns into a price check it can act on. total spend came to seventeen cents, settled onchain, with a transaction hash for every call.

a wallet is what turns an agent demo into a finished job.

your AI agent can hold a wallet and move money across chains while it works, with no person approving each transaction. 
next week join @rodventures from @edgeandnode, @rowlandgraus from @Chainlink and Alex Kopferschmitt from @TRMLabs for a free webinar on compliance in the agentic era. 
we'll walk through how policy gets enforced end to end: compliance screening, spending limits, identity, audit trails.

Frontier inference nows runs $11 per task. An agent doing real work executes 500 to 1,000 tasks a day, which puts its annual inference bill between $2M and $4M, for one agent, before a single dollar of revenue. An agent that cannot fund its own cognition is not autonomous, it is subsidized. Solving this is the actual frontier of the agent economy.
